Bearing 315

The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest
March/April 1999

[Image: Graphic containing a square divided diagonally, with the bottom-right half shaded, alongside the text "Upcoming Events"]

Upcoming Events

Woodland Park Newcomers' Meet

Rick Hood (COC)

On Saturday, April tenth (10th), at Woodland Park in Seattle, Cascade OC will host a newcomers' event. On the same day in the Columbia River OC will host a similar event in the Vancouver/Portland area. We encourage other clubs to consider hosting a newcomers' event on the same day, such that we can leverage and coordinate publicity for a "Try O" type day.

While COC has hosted an event of this nature for past few years, we are not suggesting that other clubs adopt our approach, though they are welcome to, but rather simply tailor in some fashion an event that would be especially attractive to newcomers.

I will again be the meet director for COC's event, on Saturday the 10th, and would be most interested in sharing information with others that are interested in a regional "Discover O" type day.

Producing a Local O' Meet — A How-To

Harvey Friedman (COC)

As a certificated Level I & 2 COF controller for the past ten years, I propose a course to instruct Cascade members on what is optimal for a local meet, including preparation, publicity, meet workers (number of, and specific duties for each), post meet cleanup, post meet results, publicity, etc.

I will put together information from the USOF club binder, the COF Level I controller's course, Debbie Newell's manual for local meet directors, and the over 15 years of experience I have had organizing O' events locally and participating locally, nationally, and internationally.

For 5-10 people, Monday evening 15th March from 7-10 PM at my house is a fine time. If attendees want handouts rather than just to take notes, I'll expect to recover my copying costs. For non-Cascade members, I think $50 is a fair price (or join the club, design all the courses for one meet, and be a meet director for another meet within a year of completing the course). If more than 10 total are interested, we must find a different, bigger class room. Phone me evenings between 6:30-9:30 at 206-784-2774 to sign up or for more information.

COC Officers Elected

Rick Hood (Former COC President)

The elected board members, present for COC's first business meeting of the year, elected the following individuals:

Dave Tallent, President; Eric Bone, Vice President; Ardis Dull, Secretary; Katie Sprugel, Treasurer.

Congratulations Dave, Eric, Ardis, and Katie and thanks for agreeing to serve!!! We are especially please to have a president that actively sought the position. Dave brings a wealth of experience and energy to the position. We are also pleased to have Katie Sprugel return to the board and serve as treasurer. Their term will run until the first business meeting in 2000.

So when next you see these folks at a meet, please join me, as outgoing president, and Sandy Kish Miller, as outgoing treasure, in wishing them all the best this year.

Earthworks Project

Rick Hood, COC Environmental Coordinator

On Saturday April 24th, there will be a coordinated effort to conduct trail and park projects around Puget Sound. I think this is a good fit and asked to participate at a park that we use for Orienteering. I suggested St. Ed, Hamlin, Carkeek, Lake Samm to the organizers. (There are usually about 40 parks involved. Jim Siscel, Don Denbo, and myself have participated in past years, for example.)

Anyway, there are two good ways for those in the Puget Sound area to participate. 1) let me know you are willing to spend the 24th of April helping and we will put together an O group; 2) just sign-up at any of the 40 plus park projects (I will provide a web link to in a few weeks) and when you actually sign in at the event, say you are from "xxx orienteering club" or "Washington State Orienteering Association."

Trail Work at St. Edward State Park

Jim Siscel (COC)

COC will continue to work on trails in St. Edward State Park on Saturday, April 3rd. We will be assisting an Eagle Scout candidate from Troop 304 in Lynnwood on his Eagle project. The work will be on the South Canyon trail. COC re-routed this trail several years ago out of the creek bottom to up on the hillside. The work will involve building a bulwark along the outside of the trail, recutting the inside of the trail, and leveling the trail. The work will start at 8:30am and continue until mid-afternoon. You are welcome to come and help as long as you want. Please contact Jim Siscel at 425-778-7202 if you are interested in helping.

Training

Eric Bone, COC Training Coordinator

Sunday 28 March, 2-3:30 p.m. - Orienteering technique training at Hamlin Park. Training exercises and a novice course will be offered. For more information contact Eric Bone: 206-634-2279 or ebone@u.washington.edu

Sunday 4 April, 10-11 a.m. - Surplus WIOL Champs map blowout at Magnuson Park. High school courses (3F and 3M) and the novice course will be offered. The WIOL courses had only about 25% overlap with course 4, so come experience Magnuson park anew! Try race your buddy O': Start simultaneously with someone of similar ability running the other course 3 than you. For more information call Don Atkinson: 425-488-0270

News and Notes

New O Club
Sergey Velichko (CTOC)

New Orienteering Club appeared on the Northwest USA map.

City of Trees Orienteering Club (CTOC) Serving the Boise, Idaho. You can find more information at the club's Web page: http://netnow.micron.net/~velichko/ctoc.htm. We hope to see you all at the O meets this spring, summer, and fall.

Trainee/Helper sought for Lick Creek A meet in July
Donald Denbo

This on-the-job experience will enable you to learn course design principles and have the opportunity to help design a course. Participation will require a minimum of 3 or 4 trips to the Lick Creek map to field check the courses. Interested? Contact me at 206-366-0624 or dwd@halcyon.com.

Sacajawea Orienteers 1998 Annual Report

Frank Skarina, SACO President
Ivar Husa, SACO Vice President

Sacajawea Orienteers, SACO, had a very good year. The club had an active orienteering outreach this year, primarily to school age kids. Several new maps were created and others were updated. We experimented with event formats, holding our first-ever bicycle event and first 'memory' event (no map in hand).

SACO proudly fielded their first team at the US Classic Championships, held in the summer near Cle Elum, WA. We proudly carried off our wounded as well. (Ivar DNF'd his event, with major blisters and Sonny came down with gastroenteritis). We'll do it again! (Which?)

SACO continues to attract good numbers of new people to their events, but 'regular member participation' is not so regular. In Ivar's view, we need encourage a community of orienteers here.

And we held three sessions at the YMCA:

  • 2/13: Family Fun Night (20)
  • 3/13: Outdoor Adventure Fair (40)

In 1998, Sacajawea Orienteers recorded 821 participant in their events.

Mapping also continued with changes to the Rooks Park/Bennington Lake map and the Wallula Junction map with a printing of Wallula East. Pioneer Park is ready for a printing in early 1999. We developed Camp Wooten (black and white) and started mapping Chamna near the mouth of the Yakima in Richland.

SCONET NEWS

A-meets and USOF
Olive Kearney, your SCONET booster

Look At The Wonderful Feast Of A-Meets We Have In The West This Year

Club Date Location
LAOC 6/12+ A 2 day Mt Pinos, ~90 m N of LA

NISQ/EWOC 9/18+ A 2 day Inland NW O-Cup, WA
BAOC 9/25+ A 2 day U.S. Indiv Champs, Lake Tahoe
TSN 11/6+ R 24 hr Arizona Rogaine (tentative)

(more details are available on the SCONET website where these combined schedules are housed, do take a look, you may be tempted even farther away!)

For those who have not had a chance to go to an A-meet I would recommend trying to get to them all! You will be hooked on Orienteering for ever. I include the Arizona Rogaine is becoming a must for many of us, always fun in some of those lovely remote mountain areas.

Beginners may wonder if they should participate. Never Doubt It! There are the usual White through Blue courses, with classes for all ages and Open classes. So if you are, say 30-something like me, but want to do an orange course, enter the Orange Open category.

Presidents Column

by Dave Tallent, COC President

Who is Dave Tallent?
I'm 47 years old, and am presently employed at the City of Bellevue as a Transportation Planner, where I analyze and create computer simulations. Prior to this, I taught mathematics at college.

I have many interests, including reading mathematics papers, writing them, and solving math problems. I also enjoy singing tenor in Choir of the Sound. I run, bike & swim and do karate regularly, and when not orienteering, I hike, backpack, snowshoe, climb and compete in an occasional road race and triathlon.

Over the past 14 years, I've orienteered almost 700 times, which includes almost 100 "A" meet days in the USA, Canada & Europe. I've competed in the last 13 US champs, many Western US, Canadian, Western Canadian Champs and one World Master's Championship. And I'm still not very good! I've attended 7 world cup events, and one Elite World champs. Locally, I've set courses about 30 times and been Meet Director too many times. I've been heavily involved in the High School program (WIOL) for the past 10 years, four as WIOL director. I have also been Sammamish OC president since 1988.

Times are a Changing
As many of you know, two of the clubs in WA have ceased to exist: Ellensburg OC and Nisqually Orienteers. It wasn't that their respective members didn't care or like orienteering, it was partially because the principals did not successfully "duplicate" themselves, so when they finally stepped aside their organizations could continue without them. I'm not so sure our clubs position (Cascade or Sammamish) would fare any better. Club memberships in Washington State are declining. This problem is not a localized one, but endemic throughout the US. Even ONA, our national magazine, will be closing down after July due to declining readership. Are we in the Northwest going to allow orienteering to go the way of the Dodo bird? I want orienteering to be around for our children to enjoy and hopefully theirs as well. I want orienteering to exist even when we have moved on to other activities.

My Vision.
What is my vision for orienteering in the Greater Puget Sound Region and the issues I'd like to address as President?

  • Increase our volunteer base
  • Train meet staff, course setters & meet directors, through mentoring
  • Provide quality meets
  • Create annual fixture list of events with a seasonal focus: Winter O' Series; WIOL
  • Support the High School program
  • Provide a schedule (example: two meets per month in the GPS Region)
  • Emphasize local program
  • Reduce duplication of effort between the clubs and pursue common goals

Create map groups
What can we do? We can all volunteer. Ask yourself how many times you have helped at a local meet during the past year. If every member offers to help two or three times a year, we can fully staff all our meets. This would go a long way to cut the workload of a few dedicated orienteers, prevent their "burnout," make our meets much better and give a better look to the general public. Without meet staff, there can be no meets, which ultimately means no club and no orienteering.

If you like to orienteer and want orienteering in the Greater Puget Sound, then get on the phone tonight and call me, leave a message at (425) 488-3691 or e-mail me at dtallent@gtemail.net and volunteer. Look at the current meet schedule and see what meet(s) you can work. Call the meet director. Don't wait to be asked. If you don't know how to do registrations, starts or finishes, attend Harvey Friedman's workshop, or learn on the job at a meet. If you are a regular meet worker, mentor someone at every meet, effectively "duplicating" yourself. If you are a course setter, mentor someone. If you are a meet director, mentor someone. Let's have fifty trained meet staff for 2000 and not the same twenty.

It is essential that we offer quality meets: This will require updating maps; designing appropriate courses for each ability level; visiting the area (map) when setting courses and using good standard course setting practices. (No bush whacking, swimming, or dangerous routes. No killer climbs, dog-legs or no brainer route choices etc.); hanging markers in the correct place; checking the codes; vetting the courses; having defined registration, start & finish areas; having trained meet staff and paying attention to newcomers. If we look good, people will return for more. If we look bad, participants will take their recreation dollars elsewhere!

We need a "program", a group of meets with a common focus, not just a hodge-podge of anything-goes events. This can be achieved by creating an annual fixture list, a schedule of events with a beginning and an end. Something like the public "Winter O' Series" created by Debbie Newell, or the WIOL program. How successful is this? This year there were 68 participants on course 4 for the "Winter O' Series" and between 60 & 80 high school students on course 3. That computes to between 100 & 200 participants at every meet and represents half the local clubs' annual income.

We need to support our High School program. It has been one of our greatest successes. The students may become our future orienteers and meet staff. If nothing else, the WIOL program introduces O to a larger section of the population, increases our visibility, takes our sport out of obscurity and puts us on the map.

We need to have regular meets, say one every 1st and 3rd week of each month, and not cut back just because of the lack of help. If we keep cutting back, our lack of program will speed our demise as we will not attract any new orienteers and the current membership will choose some other activity to pursue.

Our local program must be sustaining. The local program schedule must take precedence over any meets out of state. Only a few local orienteers go to Canada or California or to the US Champs. Since 80% of our local orienteers do not travel to far away places to orienteer, why should their program cease because of the few that do travel?

Putting all the above together, I suggest a combined Clubs' effort in creating an annual fixture list which has an outreach element for attracting newcomers, at four locations in the Puget Sound Region, during Sept & Oct. The Winter O' Series of 7 meets and Winter O' Puget Sound Championships in tandem with the High School league would follow from Nov to Feb. From Mar to June a Spring O' Series of 7 meets, and finally in July and August, 3 or 4 "A" or "B" meet days in the Cle Elum region, supplemented with our local low key "barbecue & easy run" Wednesday evening meets. This is a meaningful program. It is my experience that people like programs. With a program, we look good. Without a program, we look ragged and unorganized.

Finally, we need to address the problem of duplication of effort. With a current membership of a little over 100 members in all the Puget Sound clubs, I feel it's unnecessary to have multiple presidents, vice presidents, secretaries, treasurers and coordinators doing similar work. We need to unite with a common vision.

I would like to see one club with an emphasis on "map groups". For example, the "map groups" could be called N, S, E & W.

The West group would be essentially Seattle; the East group essentially Bothell, Kirkland, Bellevue etc. North could be Snohomish Co and South could be Pierce County.

The membership would align itself with the closest "map group" for the purpose of helping out at those meets, which in essence would be closer to where they live and make it much easier for them to volunteer. However, it would not mean that you could not help out or course set at any other meet you choose. The fixture list (schedule) would reflect these groupings with meets being rotated around the region. A meet could be hosted by the N followed by the E, S and W. With two meets a month, a rotation would be a two-month cycle.

This is my vision. It may not be yours. However, we all want orienteering to succeed in the Pacific Northwest. What we have been doing over the past 10 years has clearly not worked. We have been there. Let's not go back. Let's try something different. It might just work. It's your club; the board members work for you. Let's hear from you. Please E-mail with your comments and suggestions.

Rambo

January 16, 1999
Tacoma, WA
Nisqually Orienteering

Meet Director: Carl Moore
Finish Crew: Gary McCausland, Stuart Bradshaw, Sid Whaley, Mark Howlett, Dale Width
Fire and Refreshments: Ken Peters, Jan Whaley
Trophy: Sid Whaley
Marker Pickup: Ken Peters, Sve Stroosma, Carl Moore
Course: Carl Moore

Over 100 participants braved the tradition wet, blustery Rambo weather to bash through the thick woods of Point Defiance Park for Nisqually's 10th (?) Annual Rambo Score Event. Mark Howlett was crowned the Rambo King, locating all 40 controls and returning in about two and a half hours. O-veterans Skog, Urban, Bone, Veler, Waugh and Miner were close to perfect. Special mention should go to the fine turnout from CROC, a hot-bed of score-O and rogaine. Also, Bill Green came down from CHUCKO and punched them all, but suffered a severe late penalty. Dale Width was the EWOC rep.

Hats Off to the scout groups who compete well and learn as much about foul weather gear as orienteering.

A control at "S edge of marsh" was moved and partially hidden by wild animals or wind so that it was difficult to find. This affected the outcome for some, but, as they say, "That's Rambo!"

Pioneer Park

January 30, 1999
Walla Walla, WA
Sacajawea Orienteers (SACO)

Meet Director and Course Setter: Frank Skarina

Our free meet on this windy day at this 50 arce city park brought people all the way from Benton City. The mass start, the deterministic course times, and shelter from the wind precipitated community at the finish and made it very easy to find volunteers to pickup controls.

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

What equipment do I need?

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for loan (with deposit) at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense under-brush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair of socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Spring and Summer Orienteering Events

OK, the WIOL season is over, but you still want to orienteer. Well, you can! As you've probably noticed, this issue of Pine Notes is included as an insert in Bearing 315, the Cascade Orienteering Club's newsletter. As you will see when you look through Bearing 315, orienteering goes on even after WIOL is over. There are a lot of great meets coming up in the next few months in Washington and British Columbia.

Transportation to Meets

If you would like to attend a meet but don't have transportation, there are a few things you can do.

  1. Post an e-mail message to the local orienteering mail list. Here's how you do it:
    Send an e-mail message addressed to o-west@onelist.com (a mail list of local orienteers). The message will be distributed to anyone who subscribes to this list (over a hundred people, most living in the western US and Canada). Be sure to give your general location and what meet you want to attend and include a phone number or e-mail address.
  2. Subscribe to the O-West mail list and watch for messages offering rides to meets. It's easy to subscribe and doesn't cost anything. Just go to the following web site: http://www.onelist.com
    Go to the o-west mailing list (it's under sports, outdoor sports) and follow the directions to subscribe.
  3. Call or e-mail Dave Tallent (WIOL Director). He may be able to find a ride. His phone number is 425-488-3691. His e-mail address is dtallent@gtemail.net.

What is USOF?

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including costs, are fully tax deductible.

On Saturday, April tenth (10th), at Woodland Park in Seattle, Cascade OC will host a newcomers' event. On the same day in the Columbia River OC will host a similar event in the Vancouver/Portland area. We encourage other clubs to consider hosting a newcomers' event on the same day, such that we can leverage and coordinate publicity for a "Try O" type day.

While COC has hosted an event of this nature for past few years, we are not suggesting that other clubs adopt our approach, though they are welcome to, but rather simply tailor in some fashion an event that would be especially attractive to newcomers.
I will again be the meet director for COC's event, on Saturday the 10th, and would be most interested in sharing information with others that are interested in a regional "Discover O" type day.
Producing a Local O' Meet — A How-To

As a certificated Level I & 2 COF controller for the past ten years, I propose a course to instruct Cascade members on what is optimal for a local meet, including preparation, publicity, meet workers (number of, and specific duties for each), post meet cleanup, post meet results, publicity, etc.

I will put together information from the USOF club binder, the COF Level I controller's course, Debbie Newell's manual for local meet directors, and the over 15 years of experience I have had organizing O' events locally and participating locally, nationally, and internationally.

For 5-10 people, Monday evening 15th March from 7-10 PM at my house is a fine time. If attendees want handouts rather than just to take notes, I'll expect to recover my copying costs. For non-Cascade members, I think $50 is a fair price (or join the club, design all the courses for one meet, and be a meet director for another meet within a year of completing the course). If more than 10 total are interested, we must find a different, bigger class room. Phone me evenings between 6:30-9:30 at 206-784-2774 to sign up or for more information.
The elected board members, present for COC's first business meeting of the year, elected the following individuals:
Dave Tallent, President; Eric Bone, Vice President; Ardis Dull, Secretary; Katie Sprugel, Treasurer.

Congratulations Dave, Eric, Ardis, and Katie and thanks for agreeing to serve!!! We are especially please to have a president that actively sought the position. Dave brings a wealth of experience and energy to the position. We are also pleased to have Katie Sprugel return to the board and serve as treasurer. Their term will run until the first business meeting in 2000.
So when next you see these folks at a meet, please join me, as outgoing president, and Sandy Kish Miller, as outgoing treasure, in wishing them all the best this year.

On Saturday April 24th, there will be a coordinated effort to conduct trail and park projects around Puget Sound. I think this is a good fit and asked to participate at a park that we use for Orienteering. I suggested St. Ed, Hamlin, Carkeek, Lake Samm to the organizers. (There are usually about 40 parks involved. Jim Siscel, Don Denbo, and myself have participated in past years, for example.)

Anyway, there are two good ways for those in the Puget Sound area to participate. 1) let me know you are willing to spend the 24th of April helping and we will put together an O group; 2) just sign-up at any of the 40 plus park projects (I will provide a web link to in a few weeks) and when you actually sign in at the event, say you are from "xxx orienteering club" or "Washington State Orienteering Association."
Trail Work at St. Edward State Park

COC will continue to work on trails in St. Edward State Park on Saturday, April 3rd. We will be assisting an Eagle Scout candidate from Troop 304 in Lynnwood on his Eagle project. The work will be on the South Canyon trail. COC re-routed this trail several years ago out of the creek bottom to up on the hillside. The work will involve building a bulwark along the outside of the trail, recutting the inside of the trail, and leveling the trail. The work will start at 8:30am and continue until mid-afternoon. You are welcome to come and help as long as you want. Please contact Jim Siscel at 425-778-7202 if you are interested in helping.

Who is Dave Tallent? I'm 47 years old, and am presently employed at the City of Bellevue as a Transportation Planner, where I analyze and create computer simulations. Prior to this, I taught mathematics at college.

I have many interests, including reading mathematics papers, writing them, and solving math problems. I also enjoy singing tenor in Choir of the Sound. I run, bike & swim and do karate regularly, and when not orienteering, I hike, backpack, snowshoe, climb and compete in an occasional road race and triathlon.

Over the past 14 years, I've orienteered almost 700 times, which includes almost 100 "A" meet days in the USA, Canada & Europe. I've competed in the last 13 US champs, many Western US, Canadian, Western Canadian Champs and one World Master's Championship. And I'm still not very good! I've attended 7 world cup events, and one Elite World champs. Locally, I've set courses about 30 times and been Meet Director too many times. I've been heavily involved in the High School program (WIOL) for the past 10 years, four as WIOL director. I have also been Sammamish OC president since 1988.

Times are a Changing As many of you know, two of the clubs in WA have ceased to exist: Ellensburg OC and Nisqually Orienteers. It wasn't that their respective members didn't care or like orienteering, it was partially because the principals did not successfully "duplicate" themselves, so when they finally stepped aside their organizations could continue without them. I'm not so sure our clubs position (Cascade or Sammamish) would fare any better. Club memberships in Washington State are declining. This problem is not a localized one, but endemic throughout the US. Even ONA, our national magazine, will be closing down after July due to declining readership. Are we in the Northwest going to allow orienteering to go the way of the Dodo bird? I want orienteering to be around for our children to enjoy and hopefully theirs as well. I want orienteering to exist even when we have moved on to other activities.
My Vision. What is my vision for orienteering in the Greater Puget Sound Region and the issues I'd like to address as President?
Train meet staff, course setters & meet directors, through mentoring
Create annual fixture list of events with a seasonal focus: Winter O' Series; WIOL
Provide a schedule (example: two meets per month in the GPS Region)
Reduce duplication of effort between the clubs and pursue common goals

Create map groups What can we do? We can all volunteer. Ask yourself how many times you have helped at a local meet during the past year. If every member offers to help two or three times a year, we can fully staff all our meets. This would go a long way to cut the workload of a few dedicated orienteers, prevent their "burnout," make our meets much better and give a better look to the general public. Without meet staff, there can be no meets, which ultimately means no club and no orienteering.

If you like to orienteer and want orienteering in the Greater Puget Sound, then get on the phone tonight and call me, leave a message at (425) 488-3691 or e-mail me at dtallent@gtemail.net and volunteer. Look at the current meet schedule and see what meet(s) you can work. Call the meet director. Don't wait to be asked. If you don't know how to do registrations, starts or finishes, attend Harvey Friedman's workshop, or learn on the job at a meet. If you are a regular meet worker, mentor someone at every meet, effectively "duplicating" yourself. If you are a course setter, mentor someone. If you are a meet director, mentor someone. Let's have fifty trained meet staff for 2000 and not the same twenty.

It is essential that we offer quality meets: This will require updating maps; designing appropriate courses for each ability level; visiting the area (map) when setting courses and using good standard course setting practices. (No bush whacking, swimming, or dangerous routes. No killer climbs, dog-legs or no brainer route choices etc.); hanging markers in the correct place; checking the codes; vetting the courses; having defined registration, start & finish areas; having trained meet staff and paying attention to newcomers. If we look good, people will return for more. If we look bad, participants will take their recreation dollars elsewhere!

We need a "program", a group of meets with a common focus, not just a hodge-podge of anything-goes events. This can be achieved by creating an annual fixture list, a schedule of events with a beginning and an end. Something like the public "Winter O' Series" created by Debbie Newell, or the WIOL program. How successful is this? This year there were 68 participants on course 4 for the "Winter O' Series" and between 60 & 80 high school students on course 3. That computes to between 100 & 200 participants at every meet and represents half the local clubs' annual income.

We need to support our High School program. It has been one of our greatest successes. The students may become our future orienteers and meet staff. If nothing else, the WIOL program introduces O to a larger section of the population, increases our visibility, takes our sport out of obscurity and puts us on the map.

We need to have regular meets, say one every 1st and 3rd week of each month, and not cut back just because of the lack of help. If we keep cutting back, our lack of program will speed our demise as we will not attract any new orienteers and the current membership will choose some other activity to pursue.

Our local program must be sustaining. The local program schedule must take precedence over any meets out of state. Only a few local orienteers go to Canada or California or to the US Champs. Since 80% of our local orienteers do not travel to far away places to orienteer, why should their program cease because of the few that do travel?

Putting all the above together, I suggest a combined Clubs' effort in creating an annual fixture list which has an outreach element for attracting newcomers, at four locations in the Puget Sound Region, during Sept & Oct. The Winter O' Series of 7 meets and Winter O' Puget Sound Championships in tandem with the High School league would follow from Nov to Feb. From Mar to June a Spring O' Series of 7 meets, and finally in July and August, 3 or 4 "A" or "B" meet days in the Cle Elum region, supplemented with our local low key "barbecue & easy run" Wednesday evening meets. This is a meaningful program. It is my experience that people like programs. With a program, we look good. Without a program, we look ragged and unorganized.

Finally, we need to address the problem of duplication of effort. With a current membership of a little over 100 members in all the Puget Sound clubs, I feel it's unnecessary to have multiple presidents, vice presidents, secretaries, treasurers and coordinators doing similar work. We need to unite with a common vision.
I would like to see one club with an emphasis on "map groups". For example, the "map groups" could be called N, S, E & W.
The West group would be essentially Seattle; the East group essentially Bothell, Kirkland, Bellevue etc. North could be Snohomish Co and South could be Pierce County.

The membership would align itself with the closest "map group" for the purpose of helping out at those meets, which in essence would be closer to where they live and make it much easier for them to volunteer. However, it would not mean that you could not help out or course set at any other meet you choose. The fixture list (schedule) would reflect these groupings with meets being rotated around the region. A meet could be hosted by the N followed by the E, S and W. With two meets a month, a rotation would be a two-month cycle.

This is my vision. It may not be yours. However, we all want orienteering to succeed in the Pacific Northwest. What we have been doing over the past 10 years has clearly not worked. We have been there. Let's not go back. Let's try something different. It might just work. It's your club; the board members work for you. Let's hear from you. Please E-mail with your comments and suggestions.

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for loan (with deposit) at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense under-brush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair of socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Event fees are charged on a per-registration basis: two people running together with the same map and punch card pay a single fee. Additional maps are available; the charge varies depending on the map. Fees vary, but the following are typical fees for local ("C") events with color maps. "A" and "B" meets, state championships, rogaines, and other events will cost somewhat more.

The newsletter of the Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League Vol. 18 No. 3 March 1999

OK, the WIOL season is over, but you still want to orienteer. Well, you can! As you've probably noticed, this issue of Pine Notes is included as an insert in Bearing 315, the Cascade Orienteering Club's newsletter. As you will see when you look through Bearing 315, orienteering goes on even after WIOL is over. There are a lot of great meets coming up in the next few months in Washington and British Columbia.
If you would like to attend a meet but don't have transportation, there are a few things you can do.

Post an e-mail message to the local orienteering mail list. Here's how you do it: Send an e-mail message addressed to o-west@onelist.com (a mail list of local orienteers). The message will be distributed to anyone who subscribes to this list (over a hundred people, most living in the western US and Canada). Be sure to give your general location and what meet you want to attend and include a phone number or e-mail address.

Subscribe to the O-West mail list and watch for messages offering rides to meets. It's easy to subscribe and doesn't cost anything. Just go to the following web site: http://www.onelist.com Go to the o-west mailing list (it's under sports, outdoor sports) and follow the directions to subscribe.
Call or e-mail Dave Tallent (WIOL Director). He may be able to find a ride. His phone number is 425-488-3691. His e-mail address is dtallent@gtemail.net.
Check out the map samples on the next page to see what the terrain is like at some of the upcoming meets.

Mt. Tabor, Portland, OR. Newcomers Meet. Mt. Tabor is the big hill between Division and Stark, just east of 60th Ave. Enter from the north on 66th off of Belmont.
Pioneer Park, Walla Walla, WA. Newcomers Meet. Clinton St. South from Rt 12, at 2nd light turn left, 0.3 miles on right.

Riverside State Park, Spokane, WA. Exit 281 off I-90, north on Division to Francis, then west on Francis to Nine Mile Road (SR 291). Continue northwest on Nine Mile Road. Wallula Junction, Pasco, WA. East from Pasco on US 12 for about 15 miles to Wallula. Watch for O-club signs on US-12, which will direct you up a dirt road at Madame Dorian Park.

Horn Rapids, Richland, WA. Mt. Bike option. Take Highway 240, 9 miles from the "Bypass" Highway, NW of Richland or take Highway 225, 10 miles N of Benton City.

Liberty Lake County Park, Spokane, WA. Take I-90 east from Spokane (or west from Coeur D'Alene) to Exit 296 (Liberty Lake). East on Mission to Molter, then turn right and take Molter to Valley Way. Turn left and follow Valley Way onto Lakeside Road and follow the signs to the county park entrance.

As a U.S. Citizen or U.S. Resident, I hereby apply for USOF membership. I understand that I am entitled to a one-year subscription to Orienteering North America, voting rights, and membership in the IOF. Please print.
Member of a chartered club? Club ___________________

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including costs, are fully tax deductible.
